ascii_art_prohibition.statement: ASCII art diagrams are strictly forbidden in professional documentation (confidence 1.0). They are unprofessional, unreadable, break rendering, and create user frustration. MANDATORY alternatives: Mermaid (95% of cases) or SVG (5% when Mermaid has limitations). This prohibition is absolute with ZERO exceptions.
ascii_art_prohibition.source: Professional Documentation Standards, Documentation Expert, User Feedback

svg_vs_mermaid.title: Use Mermaid for Standard Diagrams, SVG for Complex Custom Layouts
svg_vs_mermaid.description: For most documentation diagrams (flowcharts, sequence, class, ERD, state, DFD), use Mermaid as it's text-based, renders professionally, and works in GitHub/GitLab/Confluence. For complex custom layouts, pixel-perfect positioning, or diagrams Mermaid cannot represent, use SVG. NEVER use ASCII art.
svg_vs_mermaid.rationale: Mermaid provides 95% of diagram needs with text-based syntax that renders as professional graphics, is version control friendly, and works across all documentation platforms. It's faster to create than hand-coded SVG and maintains the benefits of text-based diagrams (git diff, easy editing). SVG is necessary for the 5% of cases where Mermaid has limitations: complex custom layouts requiring pixel-perfect positioning, diagrams needing advanced styling beyond Mermaid capabilities, or unique diagram types Mermaid doesn't support. ASCII art is never acceptable as both Mermaid and SVG are superior alternatives.
svg_vs_mermaid.source: Documentation Expert, Mermaid Documentation, SVG Best Practices

when_not_applicable: Raster images are not SVG's purpose (use PNG/JPG for photos)
Extremely simple icons (may use icon libraries directly)

options.name: Mermaid (Default for 95% of Diagrams)
options.reason: Text-based syntax, professional rendering, universal platform support

pros: Text-based source (version control friendly, git diff works)
Universal support (GitHub, GitLab, Confluence, Notion, wikis)
Quick to create (simpler syntax than hand-coded SVG)
Living documentation (stays with code in repo)
14+ diagram types (Flowchart, Sequence, Class, ERD, State, DFD, etc.)
cons: May not support very complex custom layouts

context_requirements: Standard diagram types (flowchart, sequence, class, ERD, state)

options.name: SVG (For Complex Custom Layouts)
options.reason: Unlimited flexibility, pixel-perfect control, advanced styling

cons: Git diffs less readable than Mermaid text
May need visual editor for complex diagrams

context_requirements: Complex custom layouts Mermaid cannot represent
Unique diagram types not supported by Mermaid

options.name: ASCII Art (FORBIDDEN)
options.reason: Unprofessional, unreadable, breaks rendering, user frustration

pros: NONE - There are zero advantages to ASCII art
Any perceived advantage is better served by Mermaid or SVG
cons: Unreadable (breaks on different fonts, proportional fonts)
Not maintainable (nightmare to edit, align, update)
No styling options (monochrome, fixed size, ugly)
Breaks in modern editors with proportional fonts

context_requirements: NEVER USE - This option is forbidden
Not even as placeholder (use Mermaid instead)
Not even for quick sketches (Mermaid is just as quick)
Not even for internal docs (always professional)
If considering ASCII art, use Mermaid or SVG instead
Violation of this rule is a critical documentation failure

decision_factors: Diagram type (standard → Mermaid; custom complex → SVG)
Platform (GitHub/GitLab/wikis → Mermaid works great; standalone → either)
Team skill (developers → Mermaid easy; designers → SVG familiar)
Default recommendation → Mermaid for 95% of documentation diagrams, SVG for complex custom needs

icon_library_selection.title: Use Bootstrap Icons for Technical Diagrams, Iconoir for Modern Aesthetic, SVG Repo for Specialized Needs
icon_library_selection.description: For system/infrastructure icons (servers, databases, cloud), use Bootstrap Icons (2,000+, MIT, consistent design). For clean modern aesthetic, use Iconoir (1,671, MIT, minimalist). For specialized/unique needs, search SVG Repo (500,000+). For flowchart-specific symbols (arrows, connectors), use Flaticon.
icon_library_selection.rationale: Bootstrap Icons provides 2,000+ professionally designed, MIT-licensed icons with consistent visual language, perfect for technical diagrams (cloud, server, database, terminal, gear, etc.). The icons are well-documented, available as SVG sprites, and integrate with modern frameworks. Iconoir offers 1,671 minimalist icons with consistent 1.5px stroke width, ideal for professional documentation with clean aesthetic. SVG Repo's 500,000+ icons provide comprehensive coverage for specialized needs. This three-tier approach (Bootstrap for primary, Iconoir for style, SVG Repo for special) covers 99% of documentation diagram needs while maintaining consistency.
icon_library_selection.source: Icon Library Analysis, Professional Documentation Standards

when_not_applicable: Highly specialized domain-specific symbols (may need custom design)

options.name: Bootstrap Icons (Primary - Technical Diagrams)
options.reason: Consistent design, comprehensive technical coverage, MIT licensed, excellent documentation

pros: Technical icons: cloud, server, database, CPU, terminal, gear, shield, key, etc.
Diagram-relevant: arrows, layers, stack, grid, diagram-2, diagram-3
cons: May need supplementary library for specialized needs

options.name: Iconoir (Secondary - Modern Aesthetic)
options.reason: Clean minimalist design, consistent stroke width, professional aesthetic

pros: Perfect for documentation with clean style
cons: Smaller set than Bootstrap Icons (1,671 vs 2,000)

options.name: SVG Repo (Tertiary - Specialized Needs)
options.reason: 500,000+ icons, comprehensive coverage for any specialized need

cons: License varies by icon (check individual licenses)
May require more searching to find right icon

context_requirements: Specialized icon needs Bootstrap/Iconoir don't cover

options.name: Flaticon (Flowchart-Specific)
options.reason: Diagram-specific symbols, flowchart elements, process flow icons

pros: Data flow symbols (input, output, process, storage)
cons: Lower download limits on free tier
Less comprehensive than SVG Repo for general icons

context_requirements: Can provide attribution (free tier) or budget for Premium

decision_factors: Use case (technical diagrams → Bootstrap Icons; clean aesthetic → Iconoir; specialized → SVG Repo; flowcharts → Flaticon)
Consistency priority (high → Bootstrap Icons or Iconoir; low → SVG Repo)
Budget (free priority → Bootstrap Icons, Iconoir, SVG Repo; can pay → Flaticon Premium)
Framework integration (React/Vue → Iconoir or Bootstrap; vanilla → any)
Default workflow: Start with Bootstrap Icons → Supplement with Iconoir → Search SVG Repo for specialized needs → Flaticon for flowchart symbols
Primary recommendation → Bootstrap Icons (2,000+, MIT) for 80% of technical diagram needs

integration_method.title: Use SVG Sprites for Reusable Icons, Inline SVG for Unique Diagrams, External Files for Cacheable Assets
integration_method.description: For icons used multiple times (servers, databases, users), use SVG sprite sheets (define once, reference with <use>). For unique custom diagrams, inline SVG directly in HTML/Markdown. For large standalone diagrams, use external SVG files for caching. Never use ASCII art.
integration_method.rationale: SVG sprite sheets optimize performance for repeated icons: define symbol once in <defs>, reference multiple times with <use href='#id'/>, enables CSS styling of all instances, smaller total file size, easy to update (change symbol, all instances update). Inline SVG works best for unique custom diagrams: full control, no HTTP requests, immediate rendering, can manipulate with CSS/JavaScript. External SVG files suit large standalone diagrams: cacheable, clean HTML, versioned independently. Choice depends on reuse frequency, diagram complexity, and caching needs.
integration_method.source: SVG Best Practices, Web Performance Guidelines

when_applicable: System architecture diagrams with repeated components
when_not_applicable: Extremely small icons (icon fonts may work)
Third-party icon libraries (follow their integration docs)

options.name: SVG Sprite Sheet (for Repeated Icons)
options.reason: Optimal for multiple instances of same icons, performance benefits

pros: Smaller total file size (symbol defined once)
Easy to update (change symbol, all instances update)
CSS styling applies to all instances
cons: Requires inline SVG in HTML (symbol definitions)
All symbols loaded even if not used
Slightly more complex setup than direct embedding
Need to manage symbol IDs (avoid collisions)

context_requirements: Icons/components used multiple times in documentation
System diagrams with repeated elements (servers, databases)

options.name: Inline SVG (for Unique Diagrams)
options.reason: Full control, no HTTP requests, immediate rendering

options.name: External SVG Files (for Large Standalone Diagrams)
context_requirements: Diagram used across multiple pages (caching beneficial)

decision_factors: Reuse frequency (multiple uses → Sprite; single use → Inline; cross-page → External)
Performance priority (repeated icons → Sprite; unique → Inline; large → External)
Maintainability (repeated components → Sprite for easy updates)
Default recommendation → Sprite for repeated icons, Inline for unique diagrams, External for large standalone diagrams
